lesson.menu_desc: Past continuous — what was happening when something else happened: I was testing the system when the server crashed.
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 — Setting the Scene (Past Continuous)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 15 -- Setting the Scene

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about setting the scene with the past continuous. We use it for an action in progress in the past, the background of a story. The form is easy. Use was or were, plus the verb with i n g. I was testing the system. The team were monitoring the dashboard. Now, here is the magic. When a longer action in progress is interrupted by a short, sudden event, we use the past continuous for the long one and the past simple for the short one. I was testing the system when the server crashed. While we were monitoring the alerts, the outage started. Notice the connectors. Use when before the short past simple event, and while before the long past continuous action. So, the past continuous paints the scene, and the past simple is the sudden thing that breaks it. Try it with a real incident at work. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail about last night. So, here is what happened. At about nine, the team were watching the dashboard and everything was running fine. I was reviewing the logs when the first alert came in. While we were checking the database, the traffic spiked, and then the payment service crashed. There was a short outage. We were losing transactions, so I escalated it right away. The on call engineer was sleeping, but he recovered the service in twenty minutes. This morning, while I was writing the report, I found the root cause. An old script was running in the background and using all the memory. We are fixing it today. Call me back when you can. Thanks, Daniela. Talk soon.
